title: Kierstead et al. Question on Two-Coloring Number of Planar Graphs
statement_text: Is the two-coloring number of all planar graphs bounded by $8$?
context_text: A planar graph with two-coloring number equal to $8$ was constructed earlier, and Kierstead et al. found simpler examples yielding the same lower bound — namely, any 5-connected triangulation in which the degree-5 vertices are non-adjacent. They then asked whether the two-coloring number of all planar graphs is at most $8$.
paper_contribution: Proves that the two-coloring number of any planar graph is at most $8$ (Theorem 1), answering the question affirmatively and establishing that the bound is optimal.
notes: The question is stated in prose in the introduction without a labelled theorem environment; the paper resolves it completely. Reference is SIAM J. Discrete Math. 23 (2009), 1548–1560.
paper_title: Planar graphs have two-coloring number at most 8
